author: @gazzaka

author: @AlexAnteroLammikko
text:
"he is a man of definite opinions, which he will repeat several times"
Summarizes my biggest issues with Thunderf00ts videos to be honest. I watch from time to time and enjoy most of his videos........until about half way through or so. Usually around then it just gets too repetitive and too boring to be honest. 
He has the most important thing down: being right. But in all other avenues he needs work in his youtube game. 
But, YT is not his main job. He has always struck me as a guy who does YT as a hobby and just so happened to get a million subs because he has been in the game for so long but he clearly hasn't put a whole lot of effort into audio quality, video quality, script writing, conciseness of points, etc, etc and it means his videos often drag and repeat the same point. And usually he doesn't so much try to prove his point as express it as self evidently true and then laugh at the other position for being self evidently false. Which, often, it is but you rarely convince people with that kind of argument.

author: @toshi-ki6016
text:
POLITENESS ON THE INTERNET NECESSARY FOR OPEN DISCUSSIONS
Thunderf00t  IS  harsh ... at least on the internet. I wished he rather focus on the issue than mix up unnecessary insults with his comments. 
The inability to separate insults, in words or in the tone of voice, from views is a major problem on the internet as it raises barriers to honest discussion and looking at the facts. 
And not being face-to-face, as on the internet, seems to have reduced those elements of civility and respect people should accord each other even when they have dissenting views.
At least Matt Farrel corrected himself, if maybe not enough? 
Otherwise, we need to be responsible to ourselves and each other in ensuring we ourselves check our sources and information accuracy. 
Democracy requires we self-check before we share it ... free-speech is NO excuse not to check ourselves. 
And if we really want the internet to be a safe place for discussion, we must all be polite in our disagreements!

author: @slicko_di_poo
text:
If you want more subscribers (assuming views convert to subscribers at some reasonably fixed percentage), probably the way to do it would be to start a series of "Thunderfoot Busted" videos, and hope some kind of internet war breaks out between some folk.

(Not so easy to do, though, since although he does a lot of debunking, I don't think he does a lot of "Bunking" of his own, so doesn't expose himself to criticism. He's all about what's wrong with others - wrong-others, probably, but still, all about what's wrong with them. Easy game to play - especially if he's the teenager in that kindergarten. For someone else to come along and play it with him, they'd have to have the ability to dig up his research and pour scorn on it, so that's not likely to happen - until someone experiments with collecting YouTube views that way, I'd guess.)

author: @jsmdnq
text:
I'm pretty sure Matt Ferrel is a pump/hyper for companies. He's told what to pump/hype and gets paid to do so. There are a few of these guys around and essentially they are to hype products to get investors(although they might not realize that is what they are really doing) and to make the technology seem more legitimate. That is, much of the tech will never be practical due to actual real world limitations and we've seen these type of scams going on for centuries. With modern science it is much easier to pull these scams off because of the knowledge required to understand the details requires, generally, many years of study. The people doing these scams are not, generally, imbeciles and more than not it is MBA's that are using some scientists experiments and extrapolating it with a great heaping of hyperbole to get investors. It's a sort of advanced form of free energy.

You will find a lot of these types of scams now days on youtube. 1. They use bots to pump up their channels to make the numbers look much larger. These involve fake accounts that post fake comments and give fake subscribers. Of course the point here is to extract ad rev and to create a feedback loop(the more "popular" one seems the more the algo's will spread them to the masses making them more popular. It's the "fake it until you make it" BS). 2. Many work to extract donations and such and actually involve teams rather than 1 or maybe 2 people. These teams are hidden or the work is contracted out. There may be an entire team behind the person when it is made to look like it's just one individual.

Things are not what they seem any more and the amount of insanity going on due to all the manipulation is mind boggling. It happens anywhere there is money or power and like all things it has become very perfected.

It is really bad in finance, MSM, and military which seems to be where the bulk of these scams and techniques originated. Once you start to see how they work and the underlying "templates" you start to see it everywhere. It seems to be an exponentially growing thing.

That said, I do listen to Matt Farrah, but have not, till now, heard of Thunderfoot. What I observe is Matt brings up technology’s that Could play out.  Yes those things should be viewed with a skepticism, but with an optometric, and balanced view as well.  Just my thoughts.
BTW, all YouTube titles are click bate in one way or another, seems to be the way it looks to me anyway.  Imho, a healthy debate, and critical thinking are a good thing.  So it will be fun to see this play out.
Cheers, have a great day.

Now thanks to Thunderfoot, I am particularly wary of new technologies where everything is explained with CG animations and no other basis for believing what they are saying. As others have said, you should look into what Thunderfoot has to say about Elon Musk too. He takes Starlink apart and shows that it's not an affordable network. author: @GlevoTec
text: Thunderf00t can be harsh, but he is straightforward and what he says is mostly right. He has a wide understanding of the science and watching his videos is a great way to recognise a lot of the repetitive bullsh*t other youtube channels push out as groundbreaking, life-changing tech. It is important to fact-check what you present. Matt has mastered to make a popular format for a wide audience, but most of the viewers will just accept what he says as true, because of the size and trustability of his channel. That's why it's good that people like thunderf00t can call some stuff out. I feel kinda bad for Matt with so much pressure on him, but in this field facts and knowledge is what it's all about

replies.author: @TheAllMightyGodofCod
replies.text:
Well, I think that is fine when he goes after pseudo science or charlatans but is Matt one of those? No.
What must people forget is that Matt's channel isn't for scientists, it is for normal people, like me, who have an interest on science but don't have a formal education on science or just a slight one... In short, amateurs, in the true sense of the word and also so people starting their road into real scientific knowledge.
If we go after those people, well intentioned people, are we gaining or losing? If Matt admits defeat and stops making videos, how many people will stop or loose interest in the persue of scientific knowledge and tech?
Matt is like an introduction drug, like hardy potter or children's books are for literature, he is an introduction to.
I would say you have Matt and others doing that at the introductory level, then people like Joe Scott, Action Lab etc at an intermediate level and then people  like Veritassium, Steve Mould, Robert here or in the particular case of electricity, Mehdi and BigCliveDotCom.
Going after Matt for not always having stuff right would be like Cristiano Ronaldo trashing a teenager playing at the local football ring because "he's technic is off"

author: @deadduck8307
text:
When it comes to new battery tech, I don't think anyone is as genuine as this channel is -- there's a lot of trial and error and sometimes you stumble on something good.
Matt's channel basically glorifies that process with CGI and other fakery -- his weakness comes from a general laziness. You don't need the actual breakthrough, just the jargon and CGI to make the video and profit.
Thunderfoot's channel is basically the modern day's collection of quotes like 'There will never be a need for every household to have a personal computer.' 999 times out of 1000, he's right, but his weakness is that the 1 time he's wrong is what he'll be remembered for, just like the dude who said the above quote.

author: @samgordon9756
text:
Matt Ferrell is definitely a clickbait YouTuber. I don't know anything about the hydrogen thing because I don't think it's a promising technology.

That said, thunderfoot is completely wrong about the claims being made about the structural battery.

It is not, according to the research paper, normal batteries in carbon fiber. It's carbon fiber as both battery and load bearing element.

Thunderfoot's video was so much insane hyperbole on the matter in the first 4 minutes, it seemed obvious that he either didn't understand the paper, or the paper was the worst attempt at vaporware ever.

Turns out the paper isn't all that problematic. I need to be clear, I don't know if the science in the paper is accurate, practical, or scalable. The paper reads a bit like a sales pitch. So there's reason to consider it sus.

But the paper absolutely does not say what thunderfoot claims it does. Given the profit motive behind misinformation on the internet, it's best to treat anyone who is as blatantly wrong as thunderfoot's first 4 minutes on the batteries with the same suspicion as one ought to treat anything Ferrell produces.

Both are mostly hype. Up to now, this channel has been better than both. Don't stoop to their level in pursuit of subscriptions, please.

replies.author: @samgordon9756
replies.text:
P.S.: always read the f###!ing paper when you hear about something really cool and new from science news.

I almost never get the math, but I usually get the basics of the claims. The claims are usually far less exciting than the news reporting would have.

(Except in cases of really BFD news, which they tend to miss on the low side of importance. Probably because the big deals don't sound exciting in layman's terms.)

author: @f.d.6667
text:
*Matt Ferrel is not in the science reporting business - he's in the RELIGION business (and quite successfully so):* after watching a couple of Matt Ferrel's videos, I quickly realized that he is merely exploiting the need of the climate-scared crowd for some feel-good news that's loosely connected with something that SOUNDS like science to them. As information is a "product" like any other, you need to deliver it together with what's called a "RTB" in marketing, a reason-to-believe. As Matt has chosen the rather large target group of the "not-so-smart" (around 83-89% of the population), it's no coincidence that "being scared by the news / by the weather / etc." and believing Matt's "scientific" explanations are key characteristics of his subscribers. *For them, listening to Matt is not a way to learn about the world or to improve their ability to think for themselves, they are merely looking for a non-complex spiritual experience that gives them hope for the future.* 
...
In a way, Matt CAN'T link his good news to real facts, as this would destroy the function of his quasi-religion (hope) and hurt the core principles of any good religion which is self-reference as a way to set itself apart from the (scary / complex) reality.

author: @gregculverwell
text:
I've only watched Matt a few times - just not my cup of tea.
Thunderfart is way worse. I see him as a borderline crank. While his debunking videos can be quite valid, he can also be catastrophically wrong. And he then proceeds to repeat his incorrect assessment ad nauseum. Somehow he thinks that by repeating the same thought 10 times in slightly different ways, that makes it all the more persuasive.
I remember a few years ago when he developed a visceral hatred for Elon Musk and decided to 'debunk' SpaceX with 30 of utter nonsense. He claimed that he was proving that reusing rocket engines was uneconomic.
Of course in the years since we have seen how wrong he was. I have put him on my 'do not recommended' list. The aggravation is not worth the few good bits.

author: @C-M-E
text:
😁I wondered how long it would be before someone called Matt out. He's a good publicist, but a scientist/inventor he is not.

author: @dalemsilas8425
text: Thunderfoot is a likeable guy but a bit of a haymaker and has made huge mistakes of his own in the past like the NASA Mars helicopter which he "debunked" before the thing flew a year later... on Mars.

replies.author: @tonyh6309
replies.text:
Err, no - its you that made the 'huge mistake'. Watch the video again. He was skeptical about its viability and went through all the considerable problems with it. But towards the end of the video he concludes that it was feasable but risky, which was pretty much NASA's own view as shown at 17:49:


'As a technology demonstration, the Mars Helicopter is considered a high-risk, high-reward project.'

He followed that by saying: 


"But it all checks out. There is nothing clearly stupid about it. So yeah from immediate skepticisim about this NASA helicopter, after running the numbers it more or less checks out."

Then watch his follow up video three years later addressing the likes of you who accused him of debunking the Mars helicopter, which he most certainly did not do.

It would be interesting to see the list of  other 'huge mistakes' you claim he has made. Sure he gets some things wrong from time to time but I don't recall any that were "huge". He speculated on the cause of a Space X rocket failure, which he probably got wrong, but at the time the only data available to observers outside of Space-X was the very limited video footage.

I believe Scott Manley called it right as an engine failure; Thunderfoot definately came over as over confident with his hypothesis of what went wrong - but it was clearly all speculation given the limited evidence. Not a huge mistake in my humble opinion.

I do not know if Phil really dislikes Elon or if it is just an act for clicks, whichever it is his fans can get very hostile so I don't watch him much nowadays.

replies.author: @weegaz22
replies.text: Apparently 1M+ people disagree with you on that point, I'd even argue its exactly what people want, a lot of people got sick of all the blatantly obvious kickstarter scams using pseudoscience and people's ignorance on the subject matter and TF simply started taking them out 1 video at a time in his typically overly sarcastic way while also showing how ridiculous some of their claims were.

replies.author: @islandsedition
replies.text:
@weegaz22 I get that. I also appreciate that folk like TF do call bunk on false or poorly researched claims. 

That said, it would probably be more constructive if he used his insight to show how these ideas/tech could be improved or made to do what they claim, even if it is just to show how impractical it is to make the necessary changes. Very occasionally he does this, but really he seems more intent on calling someone an idiot/something stupid rather than being a "good professor" and guiding potential students to do better... Not to save their egos, but to improve science and science reporting. 

Robert is an exemplar at this, calling out rubbish, pointing to the flaws, but also showing what it would take to make something work.

I think TF is pretty much the epitome of the UK attitude of s**ting on others rather than encouraging them to do better. Which is ironic given he works in a country that often goes too much the other way.

author: @teslar1
text: I like Matt even though I’ve noticed errors in his work. Hopefully some good will come out of thunderfoots criticising and Matt will sharpen up his facts and improve research. He’s good at presenting new ideas in a positive way. But doing some fact checking would help

replies.author: @mt-mg7tt
replies.text: I agree. Thunderf00t is right to point out Matt's mistakes, but I' do think Matt's enthusiasm is good if it doesn't go overboard.

author: @Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
text: Ive not seen Matt but Thunderfoot has a very scientific perspective and has called out many major players for massaging the truth, including the Hyper tunnel. With regard to subscribers, there is a well known American "media star" with a reputed million subscribers in court now with remarks he made over the sandy foot shootings. Just because you are famous doent mean you right.

author: @MrBrelindm
text:
Robert, if you liked Thundefoot, then you're going to love Professor Dave Explains. Da e takes down all kinds of specious content creators. Some of his videos are guide long as well. But they are excellently done videos.

Now for a recommendation for a very good educator and content creator: Practical Engineering is hosted by a fellow named Grady and his videos are fascinating indeed!

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: Professor Dave Explains is often out of his depth and has been debunked himself for not understanding what he tried to debunk.

replies.author: @AzimuthAviation
replies.text: @Nuts-Bolts I have not found that to be the case in most of his presentations, Where I have expertise in physical science he's spot on.

replies.author: @nullgod
replies.text: @Nuts-Bolts lol just like Phil.  :D

replies.author: @Randrew
replies.text:
I'll recommend Ben at the Applied Science channel.

He doesn't tear apart other people.  He doesn't try to sell you things or ideas.  He just makes amazing things and explains how he did it and how it works.

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
Thunderfoot peddles outage (even if he's usually correct and generous with the thermodynamics)
Matt peddles feel-good excitement for the climate-panickers by regurgitation of whatever is the latest marketing wank.
I'll occasionally watch for the entertainment but that time would be of greater value if I were to DO just about anything else.

author: @ProlificInvention
text: I'm with Matt Ferrel, Thunderfoot has made a popular channel based on being skeptical which is fine-but I prefer Matt Ferrels positivity and explanations.

replies.author: @someoneelse1904
replies.text: Even if what he’s peddling is false?

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: @someoneelse1904 It may be a dead end technology and he may have gotten some details wrong but overall it was still informative.  I am glad to hear Thunderfoot correct his mistakes, and I'm sure Matt will make another follow up video.

replies.text: Wouldn’t you also want factually correct information?
